title: Upper GI Bleed — Variceal
oneLiner: Acute hemorrhage from esophagogastric varices secondary to portal hypertension; a medical emergency in cirrhotic patients with high mortality.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic Vignette:**  
A 55-year-old man with known alcoholic liver disease (jaundiced, spider naevi, ascites on exam) is brought to ED after vomiting a pint of bright red blood. He is tachycardic (HR 115), hypotensive (BP 85/50), and confused. His wife says he has been drinking heavily and stopped his propranolol. On exam, you note palmar erythema, caput medusae, and shifting dullness. He has not passed stool today, but the rectal exam shows melena.

**Pattern Clues Dissected:**
- **Known liver disease / stigmata of chronic liver disease** → cirrhosis with portal hypertension → varices are the bleeding source until proven otherwise.
- **Bright red hematemesis + hypotension + tachycardia** → active arterial or variceal bleeding (not just slow ooze); high risk of decompensation.
- **Confusion / altered GCS** → concurrent hepatic encephalopathy (precipitated by blood in gut) or hypovolemic shock affecting cerebral perfusion.
- **Stopped beta-blocker** → loss of primary prophylaxis against variceal bleeding – a classic trigger.
- **Melena on rectal** → confirms upper GI source; blood has had time to transit through bowel.

**Atypical Presentations:**
- **No obvious stigmata of liver disease** – a young patient with non-alcoholic steatohepatitis (NASH) who has never been jaundiced. You may miss the cirrhosis unless you look for subtle signs (spider naevi, firm liver edge).
- **Only melena, no hematemesis** – bleeding from gastric varices or slow ooze from esophageal varices. Patient may be oligosymptomatic until syncope.
- **Painless hematemesis with normal vital signs** – early compensated variceal bleed; easily dismissed as “small tear” – do not be fooled. Urgent endoscopy is still indicated.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Definition:**  
Variceal hemorrhage is the rupture of dilated submucosal veins in the esophagus or stomach (varices) that develop as collateral vessels due to portal hypertension. It is the most lethal cause of upper GI bleeding, with a 6-week mortality of 15–25% despite modern therapy.

**Why It Matters:**
- **High stakes:** Each episode of variceal bleeding carries a 30–50% risk of rebleeding within 6 weeks if not managed definitively.
- **Common pitfall:** Mistaking it for a peptic ulcer bleed leads to delayed endoscopy and inappropriate use of PPI monotherapy – varices require specific hemostatic interventions (band ligation, drugs like terlipressin).
- **Frequency:** ~50% of patients with cirrhosis will develop varices; 30–40% of those will bleed. It is the second most common cause of upper GI bleeding after peptic ulcer but carries the highest mortality.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**The Story:**  
Portal hypertension (portal venous pressure >10 mmHg) – most commonly from cirrhosis – forces blood to seek alternative outflow through connections between the portal and systemic venous systems. The most clinically significant collaterals are the left gastric vein (coronary vein) → esophageal and gastric varices. These thin-walled, high-flow vessels are prone to rupture when the portal pressure gradient exceeds 12 mmHg.

**Mechanism → Clinical Sign:**
- **Increased hydrostatic pressure + vessel wall tension** → varices enlarge and become more “red wale” signs (red spots on endoscopy) → risk of rupture.
- **Erosion by acid reflux or food bolus** may trigger rupture, but most bleeds are spontaneous due to high pressure.
- **Hypovolemic shock** (hypotension, tachycardia) → from rapid loss of 500–2000 mL into the GI tract.
- **Confusion** → two hits: (1) hypoperfusion of brain due to shock; (2) blood in gut provides a protein load → increased ammonia → hepatic encephalopathy in patients with already impaired liver function.
- **Melena** → blood that passes through the bowel is digested by bacteria, turning black and tarry. The more proximal the bleed, the more likely melena (vs. hematochezia if massive).
- **Absence of abdominal pain** → variceal bleeding is usually painless, because it is a vascular event, not an inflammatory ulcer.

**Connect to Pattern Recognition:**  
The classic vignette of a cirrhotic with stigmata, bright red blood, and hypotension directly maps to the pathophysiology: portal hypertension → varices → rupture → hemorrhage → hypovolemia and encephalopathy.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
**By Category:**

| Category | Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Most Common** | Cirrhosis (any aetiology: alcohol, NASH, viral hepatitis, autoimmune) | Jaundice, ascites, spider naevi, encephalopathy |
| **Precipitants** | Infection (SBP), bacteremia, sepsis | Fever, abdominal pain, raised WCC |
| | Portal vein thrombosis (non-cirrhotic) | Acute onset of ascites, splenomegaly, no stigmata of chronic liver disease |
| | Excessive alcohol use | Recent binge, withdrawal tremors |
| | NSAIDs / aspirin (erosion) | Reported use; may cause erosive gastritis on top of varices |
| **Uncommon** | Schistosomiasis (periportal fibrosis) | Young patient from endemic area, massive splenomegaly, preserved liver function |
| | Budd-Chiari syndrome | Acute abd pain, hepatomegaly, ascites, abnormal liver enzymes |
| | Hepatic vein obstruction (rare inherited) | No classic cirrhosis risk factors |

**Highlight:** Most common cause is **cirrhosis**. The most common trigger is a concurrent infection or alcohol binge.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic Criteria / Scoring System:**
- **Blatchford Score (pre-endoscopy):** Used to predict need for intervention. Score ≥6 suggests high risk. Components: BUN, Hb, SBP, HR, melena, syncope, liver disease, heart failure. Not variceal-specific but highly sensitive.
- **Rockall Score (post-endoscopy):** For mortality risk. Age, shock, comorbidities, diagnosis (varices = high risk), stigmata of bleeding. Useful for prognostication.
- **Child-Pugh / MELD Score:** For severity of liver disease; predicts outcomes in variceal bleed. MELD >18 = high rebleeding risk.

**Key Investigations:**

| Test | What It Shows | When You Order | How to Interpret |
|------|---------------|----------------|------------------|
| **Urgent OGD (within 12 hours of presentation, ideally within 6 h if unstable)** | Direct visualization of varices: size, red signs, active bleeding or clots | In any patient with suspected variceal bleed | **Confirming finding:** Presence of esophageal or gastric varices ± stigmata of recent hemorrhage. **Rule-out:** No varices seen = look for other source |
| **Full blood count** | Hb, platelets | Baseline; repeated every 4-6 h during active bleeding | Initial Hb may be normal due to hemoconcentration; drop later. Thrombocytopenia suggests hypersplenism (cirrhosis). Platelet count <50 = poor prognosis |
| **Coagulation profile** | PT/INR, aPTT | Baseline | INR >1.5 indicates liver synthetic dysfunction; worsens with bleeding |
| **Liver function tests** | Bilirubin, ALT, AST, ALP, albumin | Baseline | Elevated bilirubin, low albumin → poor synthetic function; high ALT may indicate active hepatitis trigger |
| **Lactate** | Tissue hypoxia marker | In any shocked patient | Lactate >4 mmol/L = severe hypoperfusion, worse prognosis |
| **Blood cultures** | Rule out SBP / sepsis | If febrile or any sign of infection | Positive culture indicates need for antibiotics; may precipitate variceal bleed |
| **Crossmatch** | Group & screen | Stat for massive transfusion protocol | Always crossmatch at least 4 units early |
| **CT abdomen (if suspicion of portal vein thrombosis / Budd-Chiari)** | Patency of portal vein, hepatic veins | Non-cirrhotic portal hypertension; failed endoscopy | Identifies thrombus or outflow obstruction |

**Pattern Recognition: Single Finding that Confirms / Rules Out:**
- **Confirms:** Esophageal or gastric varices with active bleeding or stigmata on OGD.
- **Rules Out:** Normal OGD without varices (though gastric varices can be missed – repeat or CT if high suspicion).
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (First Hour) — Resuscitate and Stop Bleeding:**
- **ABC + call senior / endoscopist** – this is a time-critical emergency.
- **IV access** – two large-bore cannulae (14 or 16G). If failed, intraosseous.
- **Fluid resuscitation** – give crystalloid (Hartmann’s / Plasmalyte) cautiously: avoid over-resuscitation (increases portal pressure). Target MAP >65 mmHg, not necessarily normal BP.
- **Blood transfusion** – crossmatch 4 units. Transfuse when Hb <70 g/L or if actively hemorrhaging. Use restrictive strategy (target Hb 70-80). Platelet transfusion if <30 x10⁹/L.
- **Vasoactive drug (start immediately)** – **Terlipressin 2 mg IV bolus, then 1 mg IV every 4 hours** for up to 5 days. (Alternative: Somatostatin 250 mcg IV bolus then 250 mcg/h infusion). WHY: Reduces splanchnic blood flow and portal pressure – buys time for endoscopy.
- **Prophylactic antibiotics** – **Ceftriaxone 1 g IV daily** (or oral norfloxacin if mild). WHY: Prevents SBP / bacteremia, which drives rebleeding. Duration 5-7 days.
- **Nurse in semi-recumbent position** – reduce aspiration risk. Consider endotracheal intubation if GCS <8 or continued hematemesis.
- **NG tube?** – generally NOT recommended pre-endoscopy; can dislodge clot and worsen bleeding. Use only if aspiration must be ruled out or if massive bleeding.

**Ward (24-72 Hours) — Definitive Hemostasis:**
- **Endoscopic therapy** – ideally within 12 hours. **Esophageal band ligation** (EBL) is gold standard. For gastric varices: cyanoacrylate glue injection. If active bleeding precludes visibility, consider temporary balloon tamponade (Sengstaken-Blakemore tube) as a bridge (rarely used now due to complications).
- **Post-endoscopy:** Continue terlipressin for 5 days total. Continue antibiotics. Continue blood transfusion targets.
- **Monitor for rebleeding** – every 4-hour vitals, Hb check, melena frequency. Signs: recurrent hematemesis, tachycardia, Hb drop >20 g/L in 24 hours.
- **Hepatic encephalopathy prevention** – lactulose 15-30 mL TDS titrated to 2-3 soft stools per day; consider rifaximin if recurrent.

**Long-term — Prevent Rebleeding:**
- **Non-selective beta-blocker (NSBB)** – **Propranolol** (start 40 mg PO BID, titrate to resting HR 55-60) or **Carvedilol** (6.25-12.5 mg once daily). WHY: Reduces portal pressure gradient.
- **Repeat EBL** – every 2-4 weeks until varices are eradicated.
- **Consider TIPS (Transjugular Intrahepatic Portosystemic Shunt)** – for selected patients who fail endoscopic + drug therapy (recurrent bleed). Also as a bridge to transplant.
- **Lifestyle** – alcohol cessation, management of underlying liver disease, vaccination for hepatitis B if applicable.
- **Liver transplant evaluation** – if decompensated cirrhosis.
- **Do NOT use PPIs routinely** – no benefit for varices; can increase risk of SBP. Only if concomitant ulcer found.

**What NOT to do (Common Pitfalls):**
- Do not delay vasoactive drugs while waiting for endoscopy.
- Do not use NG tube for lavage – it provokes bleeding.
- Do not give fresh frozen plasma (FFP) unless INR >2.5 and actively bleeding – volume overload worsens ascites and portal pressure.
- Do not stop terlipressin prematurely – minimum 5 days.
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Variceal Bleed | Peptic Ulcer Bleed | Mallory-Weiss Tear | Erosive Gastritis |
|---------|----------------|--------------------|--------------------|-------------------|
| **Pain** | Absent (usually) | Epigastric burning/gnawing | Retrosternal / epigastric after vomiting | Vague epigastric discomfort |
| **Exam** | Stigmata of CLD (jaundice, ascites, spider naevi) | Often normal; may have epigastric tenderness | Usually normal; sometimes epigastric tenderness | May have epigastric tenderness |
| **Endoscopy** | Esophageal/gastric varices ± red signs | Ulcer with visible vessel or adherent clot | Linear mucosal tear at gastro-esophageal junction | Diffuse friable, erythematous mucosa |
| **Labs** | Thrombocytopenia, prolonged INR, low albumin, high LFTs | Usually normal unless massive bleed | Normal | May be normal or low Hb only |
| **Key Differentiator** | History of cirrhosis + stigmata; no pain | Pain preceding bleed + NSAID use | History of repeated vomiting / retching | Recent NSAIDs, alcohol, or stress |
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
**Major Complication 1: Hypovolemic Shock / Death**  
- **How it presents:** Rapid deterioration – thready pulse, profound hypotension, oliguria, lactate >4, altered consciousness.  
- **Key signs:** Falling GCS, cold peripheries, MAP <60 despite fluids.  
- **What to do:** Activate massive transfusion protocol, call senior for balloon tamponade or emergency TIPS. Intubate.

**Major Complication 2: Rebleeding**  
- **How it presents:** Recurrent hematemesis / melena within 5 days – often during initial hospital stay.  
- **Key signs:** Hb drop >20 g/L over 24 hours, HR >100, fresh blood via NG (if placed).  
- **What to do:** Repeat endoscopy (if possible), consider TIPS. Give second round of terlipressin.

**Major Complication 3: Aspiration Pneumonia**  
- **How it presents:** Cough, fever, hypoxia after hematemesis.  
- **Key signs:** Crackles on auscultation, CXR infiltrate, raised WCC.  
- **What to do:** Chest physio, IV antibiotics (pneumonia coverage – e.g., co-amoxiclav).

**Major Complication 4: Hepatic Encephalopathy (from blood in gut)**  
- **How it presents:** Confusion, asterixis, drowsiness, coma within 24-48 hours.  
- **Key signs:** Elevated ammonia, inappropriately high NH3 but can be normal. Asterixis on exam.  
- **What to do:** Lactulose enemas if oral not possible; rifaximin; repeat lactulose.

**Late Presentation — "What happens if untreated":**  
- Patient may present after several days with melena only, then suddenly develop massive hematemesis, irreversible shock, multi-organ failure, and death. The "herald bleed" (small initial bleed) is often ignored – it must prompt urgent workup.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonic: VIBES** (for variceal bleed management priorities)  
- **V**olume resuscitation (restrictive)  
- **I**nfection control (antibiotics)  
- **B**lood products (Hb target 70-80, platelets if <30)  
- **E**ndoscopy within 12 h (banding)  
- **S**planchnic vasoconstrictor (terlipressin) + Shunt (TIPS) if fails

**Mnemonic: TERLIPRESSIN** (key drug – side effects)  
- **T**achycardia (can be reflex) – watch for bradycardia too  
- **E**lectrolytes (hyponatremia risk)  
- **R**enal perfusion (improves)  
- **L**iver blood flow (reduces)  
- **I**schemic events (mesenteric, cardiac) – rare but check ECG  
- **P**allor (cutaneous vasoconstriction)  
- **R**educes portal pressure  
- **E**sophageal variceal bleed – primary indication

**Clinical Pearls:**
1. **If a cirrhotic bleeds, the source is varices until proven otherwise** – do not treat as ulcer empirically.
2. **Terlipressin is contraindicated in severe hypertension, CAD, and asthma** – use somatostatin instead.
3. **A “blanchable” red spot on varices (red wale sign) = high risk of imminent rupture** – schedule urgent banding even if not actively bleeding.
4. **Do not give beta-blocker acutely** – in a bleeding patient, it worsens hemodynamics. Start only after bleed is controlled (usually 48-72 h post-endoscopy).
5. **Prophylactic antibiotics reduce rebleeding by 50%** – never skip.
6. **Gastric varices require glue (cyanoacrylate), not banding** – banding on gastric varices risks ulceration and fatal rebleed.
7. **If initial endoscopy is negative but high suspicion** – repeat after 24-48 h; gastric varices can be hidden under rugae.

**Common Mistakes to Avoid:**
- Using NG tube for lavage – precipitates rebleeding.
- Starting PPI before endoscopy – may mask a visible vessel but won’t stop variceal bleeding.
- Over-transfusing (aiming Hb >90) – increases portal pressure and rebleeding.
- Discharging patient without beta-blocker if they have cirrhosis and varices (Child-Pugh A/B).
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to Call Senior / ICU / Crash Team:**

| Parameter | Threshold | Action |
|-----------|-----------|--------|
| **NEWS2** | >5 (any parameter) | Call medical registrar / senior clinical fellow |
| | >7 or any single red (resp rate >30, sats <90%, BP <90 systolic) | Call ICU outreach / crash team |
| **Hemodynamic instability** | Persistent SBP <90 mmHg after 2L crystalloid | Call ITU for central access, vasopressors |
| **Lactate** | >4 mmol/L | Escalate to ITU for resuscitation |
| **GCS** | Drop by ≥2 points, or GCS <8 | Consider intubation, call senior anaesthetist |
| **Active bleeding despite initial measures** | Fresh blood in vomit after 2 L fluids + terlipressin + urgent endoscopy not feasible within 1 hour | Call senior gastroenterologist / interventional radiology for emergency TIPS or balloon tamponade |
| **Rebleeding in hospital** | Recurrent hematemesis / Hb drop >20 g/L within 24 h | Call on-call consultant hepatologist + discuss TIPS |
| **Coagulopathy** | INR >2.5 + active bleeding | Consider rFVIIa (rare) – call haematology |

**High-Risk Profile for ICU:**
- Child-Pugh C, MELD >18, active bleeding at endoscopy, or need for balloon tamponade. These patients should be managed in a level 2/3 setting (HDU/ICU) for at least 48 hours post-endoscopy.

## Upper GI Bleed — Variceal
**One-liner:** Acute hemorrhage from esophagogastric varices secondary to portal hypertension; a medical emergency in cirrhotic patients with high mortality.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See
**Classic Vignette:**  
A 55-year-old man with known alcoholic liver disease (jaundiced, spider naevi, ascites on exam) is brought to ED after vomiting a pint of bright red blood. He is tachycardic (HR 115), hypotensive (BP 85/50), and confused. His wife says he has been drinking heavily and stopped his propranolol. On exam, you note palmar erythema, caput medusae, and shifting dullness. He has not passed stool today, but the rectal exam shows melena.

**Pattern Clues Dissected:**
- **Known liver disease / stigmata of chronic liver disease** → cirrhosis with portal hypertension → varices are the bleeding source until proven otherwise.
- **Bright red hematemesis + hypotension + tachycardia** → active arterial or variceal bleeding (not just slow ooze); high risk of decompensation.
- **Confusion / altered GCS** → concurrent hepatic encephalopathy (precipitated by blood in gut) or hypovolemic shock affecting cerebral perfusion.
- **Stopped beta-blocker** → loss of primary prophylaxis against variceal bleeding – a classic trigger.
- **Melena on rectal** → confirms upper GI source; blood has had time to transit through bowel.

**Atypical Presentations:**
- **No obvious stigmata of liver disease** – a young patient with non-alcoholic steatohepatitis (NASH) who has never been jaundiced. You may miss the cirrhosis unless you look for subtle signs (spider naevi, firm liver edge).
- **Only melena, no hematemesis** – bleeding from gastric varices or slow ooze from esophageal varices. Patient may be oligosymptomatic until syncope.
- **Painless hematemesis with normal vital signs** – early compensated variceal bleed; easily dismissed as “small tear” – do not be fooled. Urgent endoscopy is still indicated.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)
**Definition:**  
Variceal hemorrhage is the rupture of dilated submucosal veins in the esophagus or stomach (varices) that develop as collateral vessels due to portal hypertension. It is the most lethal cause of upper GI bleeding, with a 6-week mortality of 15–25% despite modern therapy.

**Why It Matters:**
- **High stakes:** Each episode of variceal bleeding carries a 30–50% risk of rebleeding within 6 weeks if not managed definitively.
- **Common pitfall:** Mistaking it for a peptic ulcer bleed leads to delayed endoscopy and inappropriate use of PPI monotherapy – varices require specific hemostatic interventions (band ligation, drugs like terlipressin).
- **Frequency:** ~50% of patients with cirrhosis will develop varices; 30–40% of those will bleed. It is the second most common cause of upper GI bleeding after peptic ulcer but carries the highest mortality.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings
**The Story:**  
Portal hypertension (portal venous pressure >10 mmHg) – most commonly from cirrhosis – forces blood to seek alternative outflow through connections between the portal and systemic venous systems. The most clinically significant collaterals are the left gastric vein (coronary vein) → esophageal and gastric varices. These thin-walled, high-flow vessels are prone to rupture when the portal pressure gradient exceeds 12 mmHg.

**Mechanism → Clinical Sign:**
- **Increased hydrostatic pressure + vessel wall tension** → varices enlarge and become more “red wale” signs (red spots on endoscopy) → risk of rupture.
- **Erosion by acid reflux or food bolus** may trigger rupture, but most bleeds are spontaneous due to high pressure.
- **Hypovolemic shock** (hypotension, tachycardia) → from rapid loss of 500–2000 mL into the GI tract.
- **Confusion** → two hits: (1) hypoperfusion of brain due to shock; (2) blood in gut provides a protein load → increased ammonia → hepatic encephalopathy in patients with already impaired liver function.
- **Melena** → blood that passes through the bowel is digested by bacteria, turning black and tarry. The more proximal the bleed, the more likely melena (vs. hematochezia if massive).
- **Absence of abdominal pain** → variceal bleeding is usually painless, because it is a vascular event, not an inflammatory ulcer.

**Connect to Pattern Recognition:**  
The classic vignette of a cirrhotic with stigmata, bright red blood, and hypotension directly maps to the pathophysiology: portal hypertension → varices → rupture → hemorrhage → hypovolemia and encephalopathy.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)
**By Category:**

| Category | Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Most Common** | Cirrhosis (any aetiology: alcohol, NASH, viral hepatitis, autoimmune) | Jaundice, ascites, spider naevi, encephalopathy |
| **Precipitants** | Infection (SBP), bacteremia, sepsis | Fever, abdominal pain, raised WCC |
| | Portal vein thrombosis (non-cirrhotic) | Acute onset of ascites, splenomegaly, no stigmata of chronic liver disease |
| | Excessive alcohol use | Recent binge, withdrawal tremors |
| | NSAIDs / aspirin (erosion) | Reported use; may cause erosive gastritis on top of varices |
| **Uncommon** | Schistosomiasis (periportal fibrosis) | Young patient from endemic area, massive splenomegaly, preserved liver function |
| | Budd-Chiari syndrome | Acute abd pain, hepatomegaly, ascites, abnormal liver enzymes |
| | Hepatic vein obstruction (rare inherited) | No classic cirrhosis risk factors |

**Highlight:** Most common cause is **cirrhosis**. The most common trigger is a concurrent infection or alcohol binge.

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means
**Diagnostic Criteria / Scoring System:**
- **Blatchford Score (pre-endoscopy):** Used to predict need for intervention. Score ≥6 suggests high risk. Components: BUN, Hb, SBP, HR, melena, syncope, liver disease, heart failure. Not variceal-specific but highly sensitive.
- **Rockall Score (post-endoscopy):** For mortality risk. Age, shock, comorbidities, diagnosis (varices = high risk), stigmata of bleeding. Useful for prognostication.
- **Child-Pugh / MELD Score:** For severity of liver disease; predicts outcomes in variceal bleed. MELD >18 = high rebleeding risk.

**Key Investigations:**

| Test | What It Shows | When You Order | How to Interpret |
|------|---------------|----------------|------------------|
| **Urgent OGD (within 12 hours of presentation, ideally within 6 h if unstable)** | Direct visualization of varices: size, red signs, active bleeding or clots | In any patient with suspected variceal bleed | **Confirming finding:** Presence of esophageal or gastric varices ± stigmata of recent hemorrhage. **Rule-out:** No varices seen = look for other source |
| **Full blood count** | Hb, platelets | Baseline; repeated every 4-6 h during active bleeding | Initial Hb may be normal due to hemoconcentration; drop later. Thrombocytopenia suggests hypersplenism (cirrhosis). Platelet count <50 = poor prognosis |
| **Coagulation profile** | PT/INR, aPTT | Baseline | INR >1.5 indicates liver synthetic dysfunction; worsens with bleeding |
| **Liver function tests** | Bilirubin, ALT, AST, ALP, albumin | Baseline | Elevated bilirubin, low albumin → poor synthetic function; high ALT may indicate active hepatitis trigger |
| **Lactate** | Tissue hypoxia marker | In any shocked patient | Lactate >4 mmol/L = severe hypoperfusion, worse prognosis |
| **Blood cultures** | Rule out SBP / sepsis | If febrile or any sign of infection | Positive culture indicates need for antibiotics; may precipitate variceal bleed |
| **Crossmatch** | Group & screen | Stat for massive transfusion protocol | Always crossmatch at least 4 units early |
| **CT abdomen (if suspicion of portal vein thrombosis / Budd-Chiari)** | Patency of portal vein, hepatic veins | Non-cirrhotic portal hypertension; failed endoscopy | Identifies thrombus or outflow obstruction |

**Pattern Recognition: Single Finding that Confirms / Rules Out:**
- **Confirms:** Esophageal or gastric varices with active bleeding or stigmata on OGD.
- **Rules Out:** Normal OGD without varices (though gastric varices can be missed – repeat or CT if high suspicion).

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning
**Immediate (First Hour) — Resuscitate and Stop Bleeding:**
- **ABC + call senior / endoscopist** – this is a time-critical emergency.
- **IV access** – two large-bore cannulae (14 or 16G). If failed, intraosseous.
- **Fluid resuscitation** – give crystalloid (Hartmann’s / Plasmalyte) cautiously: avoid over-resuscitation (increases portal pressure). Target MAP >65 mmHg, not necessarily normal BP.
- **Blood transfusion** – crossmatch 4 units. Transfuse when Hb <70 g/L or if actively hemorrhaging. Use restrictive strategy (target Hb 70-80). Platelet transfusion if <30 x10⁹/L.
- **Vasoactive drug (start immediately)** – **Terlipressin 2 mg IV bolus, then 1 mg IV every 4 hours** for up to 5 days. (Alternative: Somatostatin 250 mcg IV bolus then 250 mcg/h infusion). WHY: Reduces splanchnic blood flow and portal pressure – buys time for endoscopy.
- **Prophylactic antibiotics** – **Ceftriaxone 1 g IV daily** (or oral norfloxacin if mild). WHY: Prevents SBP / bacteremia, which drives rebleeding. Duration 5-7 days.
- **Nurse in semi-recumbent position** – reduce aspiration risk. Consider endotracheal intubation if GCS <8 or continued hematemesis.
- **NG tube?** – generally NOT recommended pre-endoscopy; can dislodge clot and worsen bleeding. Use only if aspiration must be ruled out or if massive bleeding.

**Ward (24-72 Hours) — Definitive Hemostasis:**
- **Endoscopic therapy** – ideally within 12 hours. **Esophageal band ligation** (EBL) is gold standard. For gastric varices: cyanoacrylate glue injection. If active bleeding precludes visibility, consider temporary balloon tamponade (Sengstaken-Blakemore tube) as a bridge (rarely used now due to complications).
- **Post-endoscopy:** Continue terlipressin for 5 days total. Continue antibiotics. Continue blood transfusion targets.
- **Monitor for rebleeding** – every 4-hour vitals, Hb check, melena frequency. Signs: recurrent hematemesis, tachycardia, Hb drop >20 g/L in 24 hours.
- **Hepatic encephalopathy prevention** – lactulose 15-30 mL TDS titrated to 2-3 soft stools per day; consider rifaximin if recurrent.

- **Non-selective beta-blocker (NSBB)** – **Propranolol** (start 40 mg PO BID, titrate to resting HR 55-60) or **Carvedilol** (6.25-12.5 mg once daily). WHY: Reduces portal pressure gradient.
- **Repeat EBL** – every 2-4 weeks until varices are eradicated.
- **Consider TIPS (Transjugular Intrahepatic Portosystemic Shunt)** – for selected patients who fail endoscopic + drug therapy (recurrent bleed). Also as a bridge to transplant.
- **Lifestyle** – alcohol cessation, management of underlying liver disease, vaccination for hepatitis B if applicable.
- **Liver transplant evaluation** – if decompensated cirrhosis.
- **Do NOT use PPIs routinely** – no benefit for varices; can increase risk of SBP. Only if concomitant ulcer found.

**What NOT to do (Common Pitfalls):**
- Do not delay vasoactive drugs while waiting for endoscopy.
- Do not use NG tube for lavage – it provokes bleeding.
- Do not give fresh frozen plasma (FFP) unless INR >2.5 and actively bleeding – volume overload worsens ascites and portal pressure.
- Do not stop terlipressin prematurely – minimum 5 days.

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Variceal Bleed | Peptic Ulcer Bleed | Mallory-Weiss Tear | Erosive Gastritis |
|---------|----------------|--------------------|--------------------|-------------------|
| **Pain** | Absent (usually) | Epigastric burning/gnawing | Retrosternal / epigastric after vomiting | Vague epigastric discomfort |
| **Exam** | Stigmata of CLD (jaundice, ascites, spider naevi) | Often normal; may have epigastric tenderness | Usually normal; sometimes epigastric tenderness | May have epigastric tenderness |
| **Endoscopy** | Esophageal/gastric varices ± red signs | Ulcer with visible vessel or adherent clot | Linear mucosal tear at gastro-esophageal junction | Diffuse friable, erythematous mucosa |
| **Labs** | Thrombocytopenia, prolonged INR, low albumin, high LFTs | Usually normal unless massive bleed | Normal | May be normal or low Hb only |
| **Key Differentiator** | History of cirrhosis + stigmata; no pain | Pain preceding bleed + NSAID use | History of repeated vomiting / retching | Recent NSAIDs, alcohol, or stress |

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?
**Major Complication 1: Hypovolemic Shock / Death**  
- **How it presents:** Rapid deterioration – thready pulse, profound hypotension, oliguria, lactate >4, altered consciousness.  
- **Key signs:** Falling GCS, cold peripheries, MAP <60 despite fluids.  
- **What to do:** Activate massive transfusion protocol, call senior for balloon tamponade or emergency TIPS. Intubate.

**Major Complication 2: Rebleeding**  
- **How it presents:** Recurrent hematemesis / melena within 5 days – often during initial hospital stay.  
- **Key signs:** Hb drop >20 g/L over 24 hours, HR >100, fresh blood via NG (if placed).  
- **What to do:** Repeat endoscopy (if possible), consider TIPS. Give second round of terlipressin.

**Major Complication 3: Aspiration Pneumonia**  
- **How it presents:** Cough, fever, hypoxia after hematemesis.  
- **Key signs:** Crackles on auscultation, CXR infiltrate, raised WCC.  
- **What to do:** Chest physio, IV antibiotics (pneumonia coverage – e.g., co-amoxiclav).

**Major Complication 4: Hepatic Encephalopathy (from blood in gut)**  
- **How it presents:** Confusion, asterixis, drowsiness, coma within 24-48 hours.  
- **Key signs:** Elevated ammonia, inappropriately high NH3 but can be normal. Asterixis on exam.  
- **What to do:** Lactulose enemas if oral not possible; rifaximin; repeat lactulose.

**Late Presentation — "What happens if untreated":**  
- Patient may present after several days with melena only, then suddenly develop massive hematemesis, irreversible shock, multi-organ failure, and death. The "herald bleed" (small initial bleed) is often ignored – it must prompt urgent workup.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls
**Mnemonic: VIBES** (for variceal bleed management priorities)  
- **V**olume resuscitation (restrictive)  
- **I**nfection control (antibiotics)  
- **B**lood products (Hb target 70-80, platelets if <30)  
- **E**ndoscopy within 12 h (banding)  
- **S**planchnic vasoconstrictor (terlipressin) + Shunt (TIPS) if fails

**Mnemonic: TERLIPRESSIN** (key drug – side effects)  
- **T**achycardia (can be reflex) – watch for bradycardia too  
- **E**lectrolytes (hyponatremia risk)  
- **R**enal perfusion (improves)  
- **L**iver blood flow (reduces)  
- **I**schemic events (mesenteric, cardiac) – rare but check ECG  
- **P**allor (cutaneous vasoconstriction)  
- **R**educes portal pressure  
- **E**sophageal variceal bleed – primary indication

**Clinical Pearls:**
1. **If a cirrhotic bleeds, the source is varices until proven otherwise** – do not treat as ulcer empirically.
2. **Terlipressin is contraindicated in severe hypertension, CAD, and asthma** – use somatostatin instead.
3. **A “blanchable” red spot on varices (red wale sign) = high risk of imminent rupture** – schedule urgent banding even if not actively bleeding.
4. **Do not give beta-blocker acutely** – in a bleeding patient, it worsens hemodynamics. Start only after bleed is controlled (usually 48-72 h post-endoscopy).
5. **Prophylactic antibiotics reduce rebleeding by 50%** – never skip.
6. **Gastric varices require glue (cyanoacrylate), not banding** – banding on gastric varices risks ulceration and fatal rebleed.
7. **If initial endoscopy is negative but high suspicion** – repeat after 24-48 h; gastric varices can be hidden under rugae.

**Common Mistakes to Avoid:**
- Using NG tube for lavage – precipitates rebleeding.
- Starting PPI before endoscopy – may mask a visible vessel but won’t stop variceal bleeding.
- Over-transfusing (aiming Hb >90) – increases portal pressure and rebleeding.
- Discharging patient without beta-blocker if they have cirrhosis and varices (Child-Pugh A/B).

### 10. Escalation Criteria
**When to Call Senior / ICU / Crash Team:**

| Parameter | Threshold | Action |
|-----------|-----------|--------|
| **NEWS2** | >5 (any parameter) | Call medical registrar / senior clinical fellow |
| | >7 or any single red (resp rate >30, sats <90%, BP <90 systolic) | Call ICU outreach / crash team |
| **Hemodynamic instability** | Persistent SBP <90 mmHg after 2L crystalloid | Call ITU for central access, vasopressors |
| **Lactate** | >4 mmol/L | Escalate to ITU for resuscitation |
| **GCS** | Drop by ≥2 points, or GCS <8 | Consider intubation, call senior anaesthetist |
| **Active bleeding despite initial measures** | Fresh blood in vomit after 2 L fluids + terlipressin + urgent endoscopy not feasible within 1 hour | Call senior gastroenterologist / interventional radiology for emergency TIPS or balloon tamponade |
| **Rebleeding in hospital** | Recurrent hematemesis / Hb drop >20 g/L within 24 h | Call on-call consultant hepatologist + discuss TIPS |
| **Coagulopathy** | INR >2.5 + active bleeding | Consider rFVIIa (rare) – call haematology |

**High-Risk Profile for ICU:**
- Child-Pugh C, MELD >18, active bleeding at endoscopy, or need for balloon tamponade. These patients should be managed in a level 2/3 setting (HDU/ICU) for at least 48 hours post-endoscopy.
